GentLemen:
During the l-990 Prinary Election, Delphi r,ras the political
consultant for the Truth in Planning Committee in West Sacramento,
CA. The connittee $ras formed in opposition to lteasure B. Measure
B was narrowly rejected by the voters in the June 5th Election.
An event occurred just prior to the election that has finally
caused me to write the FPPC for review and clarification. 
There
are two weekly newspapers in West Sacramento, the Nehrs Ledger and
the west sacranento Press.
Both papers are publ j.shed for
distribution on wednesday of each week.
Pr.i.or, during and after the June Election, both papers have been
very outspoken on their positions concerning lileasure B. The Ledger
was supportive of Dleasure B and the PIeEE was opposed. This brings
me to my point of concern.
Both papers published and distributed their 
neb/spapers the
Wednesday before the el-ection. A11 sides knew their deadlines for
copy and ads. Hohrever, the Ledqer published the next edition of
their paper that would have been normallv distributed the day after
+-he election +-he ,eek-end baf cre the olection. 
Th j-s r.,'as j ust f our
days after their last edition and three days before their normal
tirne of distribution.
Furthermore, their paper was distributed by nail to a very large
number of households in West Sacramento. It was received by voters
on l"londay, the day before the election.
My concern and inquiry is as follows:
1. since the paper only advj.sed the supporters (the QUAD
conmittee) of Measure B about the early publication,
shoul-d their early edition (or any appropriate portion)
be consi-dered as an in-kind contribution?

2. Since the content of the special edition was an attack
on anyone and everyone opposed to Measure B without
allowing the opponents the opportunity to place ads,
should their early edition be considered as an in-kind
contribut ion?
3. Since their

their action be considered as an in-kind
4. Since there is clear evidence (the opponentst opinion
columns) the opponents were consulted about the early
addltlon cf i-he Faper, should their cooperative effort be
considered as an in-kind contribution?
While I realize there are certain fourth estate rights protected by
the Constitution, I feel the laws pertaining to political 
campaigns
should apply to everyone - including the Press. In my 28 years as
a political 
consultant, I have never seen a regularly published
newspaper turn any edition into a last nj-nute piece of political-
hit mail .
By any evaluation (I hope), the action of the News Ledcrer just
prior to the June Prinary Election cannot be consider to be
anything but a political rnailer and should be reported by the QUAD
Conmittee as an in-kind political contribution.
The Ledqer will contend that it was not a political mailer since it
contained regular advertisenent. If the records of the newspaper
are checked, it will be discovered that the advertisers were thlir
regular weekLy cL j-ents. However, if in fact they did advise their
regular advertisers and the QUAD Committee of the early addition -
why didnrt they advise the other candidates running for City
Council and the opponents to Measure B?
I Houid app,reciate your ruting cn this ir,gu:ry as I beL j-eve it has
far reaching irnplications as how newspapers can effect the outcone
of elections that transcends normal coverage and editorial-
commentary.
Please address your response
4700 Northgate
Sacrament, CA
to our new address:
B1vd. Suite 110
95834
Thank you for your tirne and attention you will give this inquiry.
S incerely ,
Herb Willians

sections.question:
1. Since the paper only advised the supporters (the QUAD committee) of Measure B about the early publication, should their early edition (or any appropriate portion) be considered as an in-kind contribution?

2. Since the content of the special edition was an attack on anyone and everyone opposed to Measure B without allowing the opponents the opportunity to place ads, should their early edition be considered as an in-kind contribution?

3. Since their early edition was mailed to voters throughout the city and received the day before the election, should their action be considered as an in-kind contribution?

4. Since there is clear evidence (the opponents' opinion columns) the opponents were consulted about the early addition of the paper, should their cooperative effort be considered as an in-kind contribution?
sections.question_synthetic: Should a newspaper's early publication of a special edition that was distributed to voters the day before an election, which selectively informed supporters of Measure B while attacking opponents without giving them advertising opportunity, be considered an in-kind contribution reportable under political campaign finance law?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a political consultant to the FPPC, not an FPPC response. Document contains four numbered questions addressing in-kind contribution concerns. No CONCLUSION or FPPC response is present in this document. Confidence reduced slightly due to OCR-quality document with some scanning artifacts in formatting.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Since the paper only advised the supporters (the QUAD committee) of Measure B about the early publication, should their early edition (or any appropriate portion) be considered as an in-kind contribution?

2. Since the content of the special edition was an attack on anyone and everyone opposed to Measure B without allowing the opponents the opportunity to place ads, should their early edition be considered as an in-kind contribution?

3. Since their early edition was mailed to voters throughout the city and received the day before the election, should their action be considered as an in-kind contribution?

4. Since there is clear evidence (the opponents' opinion columns) the opponents were consulted about the early addition of the paper, should their cooperative effort be considered as an in-kind contribution?

embedding.summary: Herb Williams, a political consultant for the Truth in Planning Committee opposing Measure B in West Sacramento, requests FPPC clarification on whether a local newspaper's early publication and distribution of a special edition favoring Measure B should be considered an in-kind political contribution subject to campaign finance reporting requirements.
